concerns.text: The plan relies on a 'fuzzy match' on alloy name and environment to merge composition (Materials Project) and corrosion (NIST) data. This introduces a massive confound: if the merge fails or is approximate, the resulting dataset will contain mismatched composition-environment pairs. The model will learn noise or spurious correlations rather than the true physical relationship, invalidating any feature importance or R² conclusions.

The plan defines 'Alloy Family' as the primary base metal (Fe, Ni, Al, Ti) for the Leave-One-Alloy-Family-Out split. This is likely insufficient to prevent data leakage. Different alloys within the same base family (e.g., SS304 vs SS316) share highly similar compositions. A model trained on SS304 can easily generalize to SS316 based on shared elemental fractions, meaning the test set is not truly 'unseen' in terms of composition space. The split strategy must be at the specific alloy level or use a stricter clustering method to ensure true generalization to new compositions.

The plan proposes a 'one-sample permutation test' with only 100 permutations to generate p-values for feature importance. For a dataset of ~500-2000 records, 100 permutations is statistically underpowered to distinguish true signal from noise, especially after Bonferroni correction. This will likely result in high Type II error (false negatives), failing to identify significant drivers even if they exist, or producing unstable p-values that do not support the claim of 'robustness'.

The plan states: 'Handle missing pH: Median imputation per alloy family.' Imputing a critical environmental variable (pH) with a median value destroys the variance required to learn the interaction between composition and environment. If pH is missing, the record's environmental context is unknown; imputing it assumes the environment is 'average,' which biases the model and invalidates the partial dependence plots for pH interactions.
